questions.question: What is the main job of a global traffic-steering layer?
options: Choose healthy and policy-appropriate destinations while balancing latency, capacity, and safety.
Replace every application-level retry policy.
Guarantee that no region ever becomes full.
Remove the need for observability.
questions.explanation: Traffic steering is about controlled route choice under changing conditions.

questions.question: Why might lowest latency not always win in routing decisions?
options: Because health, capacity, compliance, or data affinity can outweigh raw latency.
Because latency is never visible to users.
Because anycast ignores geography completely.
Because sessions cannot survive in more than one POP.
questions.explanation: Senior routing answers account for policy and overload, not just speed.

questions.question: What is route flapping?
options: Rapid switching between destinations due to noisy or unstable health inputs.
A permanent move of traffic to a new region during migration.
The use of sticky sessions for one tenant.
A cache purge sent to all POPs.
questions.explanation: Flapping is dangerous because it amplifies instability and confusion.

questions.question: Why should routing policy consider downstream capacity, not just edge health?
options: A healthy POP can still send traffic into an overloaded region and worsen the incident.
Capacity signals only matter for batch jobs.
POP health guarantees backend capacity automatically.
Capacity signals are slower than latency so they can be ignored.
questions.explanation: Routing must avoid pushing more traffic into already saturated backends.

questions.question: What is a strong reason to keep route explanations?
options: Operators need to understand which health, latency, or override input caused a routing decision.
It removes the need for metrics.
It lets clients choose any backend directly.
It guarantees compliance policy correctness by itself.
questions.explanation: Explainability is critical during incidents and policy debugging.

questions.question: Which metric best detects control-plane propagation issues?
options: Policy version skew or propagation lag across POPs.
Average logo image size.
The count of markdown headings in docs.
Only total fleet CPU.
questions.explanation: Skew tells you some POPs are acting on old route policy.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with session or data affinity?
options: It protects locality and correctness but limits how freely traffic can reroute during incidents.
It removes the need for traffic steering entirely.
It always lowers latency more than any other method.
It guarantees zero failover impact.
questions.explanation: Affinity is useful but can constrain recovery options.

questions.question: When is anycast especially attractive at the global ingress layer?
options: When you want resilient, low-latency entry into a large edge network before applying finer routing policy.
When you need every request to be pinned to one origin forever.
When control-plane updates should take hours to propagate.
When the service cannot expose health signals.
questions.explanation: Anycast shines as a resilient entry mechanism, not as the whole routing story.
